data.title: U.K. Inflation Reaches 10-Month High, Complicating Bank of England's Rate Path
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

U.K. annual inflation in January reached its highest level since March, complicating the picture for the Bank of England after it said it would cut interest rates gradually amid a weak outlook for economic growth.

Consumer prices were 3.0% higher in January than a year earlier, up from 2.5% in December, the Office for National Statistics said Wednesday. Economists had expected annual inflation to rise to 2.8% according to a poll compiled by The Wall Street Journal.

That meant inflation rose further above the bank's 2% target, suggesting the BOE will find it more difficult to bring interest rates down in the hopes of boosting an economy that grew 0.1% in the final quarter of 2024.

The bank cut its key rate to 4.5% at its latest meeting earlier in February, with Gov. Andrew Bailey saying it would take a "gradual and careful" approach to reducing rates ahead.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: GDB Wins MYR83 Million in Adjudication Case Against Dynamic Builders
data.description:
GDB secured a favorable adjudication ruling against Dynamic Builders, as the adjudicator ordered the latter to pay 82.7 million ringgit.

The decision also mandates interest payments on specific sums and 158,844.8 ringgit in adjudication costs, according to a Tuesday Malaysian bourse filing.

The ruling came after proceedings initiated in October 2024. GDB will provide further updates on material developments, it said.

data.title: IRIS Closes Sale of IRIS Tech Ventures
data.description:
IRIS completed the sale of a 100% stake in its unit IRIS Tech Ventures on Tuesday.

The company agreed to offload the unit for 2.4 million ringgit, according to earlier filings.

data.title: U.K. Inflation Reaches 10-Month High, Complicating Bank of England's Rate Path — Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

U.K. annual inflation in January reached its highest level since March last year, complicating the picture for the Bank of England after it said it would cut interest rates gradually amid a weak outlook for economic growth.

Consumer prices were 3.0% higher in January than a year earlier, up from 2.5% in December, the Office for National Statistics said Wednesday. Economists had expected annual inflation to rise to 2.8% according to a poll compiled by The Wall Street Journal.

That meant inflation rose further above the bank's 2% target, suggesting the BOE will find it more difficult to bring interest rates down in the hopes of boosting an economy that grew 0.1% in the final quarter of 2024.

The bank cut its key rate to 4.5% at its latest meeting earlier in February, with Governor Andrew Bailey saying it would take a "gradual and careful" approach to reducing rates ahead. The BOE has cut rates at a slower pace than both the Federal Reserve and the European Central Bank since the summer.

However, the uptick in inflation was expected by BOE policymakers. January's increase in inflation was a result in a jump in air fares, as well as private-school fees, which became subject to value-added tax at the start of the year.

Wage data published Tuesday also showed accelerating pay growth, which will likely knock on to inflation, particularly in the labor-intensive services sector.

Services inflation surged to 5.0% in January, up from 4.4% in December, while core prices that exclude more volatile food and energy were up 3.7%, from 3.2% in the last month of 2024.

Inflation is expected to rise even further, peaking at 3.7% by the end of summer, the bank said in its latest macroeconomic forecasts.

They were the same forecasts that slashed economic-growth expectations for 2025--to 0.75% from 1.5% previously--symbolizing the contrasting concerns of stubborn inflation and anemic growth for the bank.

Furthermore, the uncertainty of trade tariffs from the Trump administration threatens to derail attempts to put the economy on a firmer footing. BOE's Bailey said Tuesday that he would prefer less volatility from the U.S., and that fragmentation of trade was bad for global growth.

Trade concerns could also further weaken the pound against the U.S. dollar, which would push prices of energy and other imports higher. The Federal Reserve has also given no suggestion it is in a rush to lower borrowing costs, suggesting investors will stick with the currency offering higher yield returns.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com

data.title: SWHY Distributes Nearly 26% Stake in Shenwan Hongyuan to Shareholders
data.description:
SWHY BVI has distributed 402,502,312, or 25.78%, shares in Shenwan Hongyuan (H.K.) to its shareholders in specie, a Tuesday bourse filing said.

Of this, 15.68%, 5.14%, and 4.96% of the shares have been transferred to SWHY International, SIIC Finance, and First Shanghai Investments , respectively.

Following the transfer, SWHY International directly holds 1,013,131,792, or 64.9%, shares, in Shenwan Hongyuan (H.K.)'s total issued share capital.

SWHY, which owns the three firms to which the shares have been transferred through Shenwan Hongyuan Securities, remains Shenwan Hongyuan's ultimate controlling shareholder.

data.title: UK's Annual Producer Input Prices Fall in January; Output Prices Up
data.description:
The UK's producer input prices fell 0.1% year over year in January, following a revised 1.3% decline in the previous month, the Office for National Statistics said Wednesday.

Analysts expected a decrease of 0.5% for producer input prices.

Meanwhile, producer output prices edged up 0.3%, against a revised 0.1% drop earlier and the consensus estimate of a 0.1% gain.

On a monthly basis, producer input prices were 0.8% higher, matching analyst expectations. Output prices rose 0.5%, against the market forecast of a 0.2% jump.

data.title: Eurosports Global Repurchases Shares
data.description:
Eurosports Global bought back 50,000 shares at SG$0.158 each, totaling SG$7,947, according to a Tuesday filing on the Singapore Exchange.

Since commencing its buyback mandate, the sports and lifestyle products company has repurchased 938,100 shares.

data.title: British Annual Retail Prices Climb 3.6% in January
data.description:
The UK retail price index rose 3.6% year over year in January, after a 3.5% increase in the previous month, data from the Office for National Statistics showed Wednesday.

The latest reading is below the consensus estimate of a 3.7% gain.

On a monthly basis, the index was down 0.1%, matching analyst expectations, against the prior 0.3% jump.

data.title: RBC Cuts Formycon's Price Target on Portfolio Setbacks, Profitability Risks
data.description:
RBC Capital Markets lowered its price target on Formycon to 51 euros from 63 euros, citing deeper-than-expected price discounting for FYB202, the company's biosimilar version of Johnson & Johnson's Stelara.

The research firm maintained its outperform rating on the stock.

Subsequently, RBC slashed its FYB202 peak sales estimate to 254 million euros from 449 million euros, reducing its per-share valuation to 16.9 euros from 23.4 euros. Analysts also noted the German biosimilar developer's decision to pause the US commercialization of Cimerli, a Lucentis biosimilar, due to pricing difficulties.

"Overall, our DCF / SOTP derived PT falls from 63 euros to 51 euros. This still indicates attractive upside after a major share price correction, but we also see more risk that Formycon may not be sufficiently financed to reach sustainable profitability," RBC wrote.

On a positive note, analysts expect Formycon to save significant research and development expenses over the next few years after scrapping one of two trials for FYB206, a Keytruda biosimilar, following discussions with the US Food and Drug Administration.

Looking ahead, RBC now expects Formycon to report a revenue of 33.1 million euros in 2025 and 63.1 million euros in 2026, a sharp decline from its prior forecast of 78 million euros and 210 million euros, respectively.

data.title: Singapore Exchange Set to Benefit From Measures to Boost Local Equities — Market Talk
data.description: Singapore Exchange will likely benefit in the short to medium term from government measures aiming to boost the local equities market, CGS International analysts say in a note. Tax incentives were introduced in the Singapore budget announced Tuesday. These include tax rebates and concessionary tax rates for companies looking to list on SGX, as well as tax incentives for fund managers that invest substantially in Singapore-listed equities, they write. These measures could raise net new money inflows, local market trading turnover, liquidity and overall regional interest in the Singapore market, they say. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Nordic Stocks Tipped to Open Flat — Market Talk
data.description: Nordic markets may open little changed with IG calling the OMXS30 flat at around 2757. U.S. stock markets moved cautiously higher yesterday after Monday's holiday for Presidents Day. President Trump voiced new tariff threats yesterday and mentioned, among other things, that there will probably be tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. "This has led to a somewhat more mixed development in Asia." The U.S. and Russia met yesterday in Saudi Arabia for a first meeting on peace in Ukraine. Trump expressed optimism after the meeting and said that they have agreed on more meetings in the future, but the road to peace will probably not be easy, SEB adds. OMXS30 closed at 2757.28, OMXN40 at 2644.78 and OBX at 1409.98. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: ASR Nederland's Results Could Lift Shares Slightly — Market Talk
data.description: ASR Nederland's results should have a moderately positive impact on its shares, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note after the Dutch insurer's 2024 print. The group's organic capital generation for the second half was 5% higher than consensus expectations, analyst Michele Ballatore writes. "The 5pps beat on Solvency (198%) is also a positive development, with markets impact possibly looking lower than expected," he notes. He also flags the positive development in the non-life combined ratio and good momentum in the defined contribution space in the life segment. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Chatter: Taiwanese AI Server Manufacturers Eye Expansion in Texas
data.description:
Taiwanese AI server manufacturers are eyeing Texas for major investments in May, following U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats, Taipei Times reported Wednesday, citing Taiwan Electrical and Electronic Manufacturers' Association (TEEMA) Chairman Richard Lee.

A delegation of seven firms recently visited U.S. states, and Texas emerged as a top choice for expansion. Lee noted that a major AI server foundry will announce its Texas expansion soon, according to the report.

Taiwanese firms aim to strengthen ties with the U.S. through increased investments, particularly in response to supply chain shifts. TEEMA also plans AI-focused forums and greater participation in U.S. trade events, the publication said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Georgia Jobless Rate Falls to 14.2% in Q4
data.description:
The unemployment rate in Georgia declined to 14.2% in the fourth quarter of 2024, from 15.3% in the same period last year, as the number of unemployed persons decreased by 14.7 thousand, equalling 233.9 thousand.

The jobless rate for men was down to 16.7% (vs 16.8% in Q4 2023) while that for women also fell to 11.1% (vs 13.5%).

Meanwhile, the employment rate increased to 47.1% in the December quarter from 48.9% a year ago, with the number of employed persons rising by 39.8 thousand to 1,412 thousand.

The labor force participation rate rose to 54.9% from 55.5% in the previous year.

data.title: Integrum Mulls Spinning off Research, Development Pipeline Under New Strategy+
data.description:
Integrum (INTEG-B.ST) said Tuesday it is assessing various strategic options for its long-term research and development pipeline, including a potential demerger.

The Swedish medical technology company believes spinning off all or parts of its R&D pipeline will pave the way for a more distinct valuation of its business components while separating long-term R&D investments from commercial operations.

The review is part of the company's new strategy to expand the reach of its OPRA Implant System. The strategy involves streamlining its operations to generate annual cost efficiencies of up to 20 million kronor.

data.title: Market Chatter: ASE Opens Malaysia Facility to Double AI Capacity
data.description:
ASE Technology unit ASE inaugurated its fifth plant in Penang, Malaysia, to double its IC packaging and testing capacity for AI and auto electronics, Focus Taiwan reported Tuesday.

The new facility expands ASE's production space from 92,903 to 315,870 square meters, boosting its workforce from 3,300 to over 6,000, according to the report.

ASE aims to support global clients in robotics and power management chip development. The company also continues capacity expansion in Japan, South Korea, and the Philippines, the publication said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: TTK Prestige to Invest INR5 Billion Over Three Years
data.description:
Kitchen appliances manufacturer TTK Prestige plans to invest billion Indian rupees over the next three financial years, beginning in the fourth quarter of 2025.

The investment aims to strengthen the company's core business in cookware and kitchen appliances, according to a filing with the Indian stock exchanges on Tuesday.

The company's board has approved the allocation of these funds, with 2 billion rupees earmarked for operational improvements. The remaining 3 billion rupees has been allocated for capital expenditure.

TTK Prestige anticipates the plan may temporarily impact operating earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization margins over the next eight quarters due to initial one-time costs, TTK Prestige said in the filing.

data.title: CPH Group Appoints New CEO
data.description:
Swiss chemicals and paper products maker CPH Group named Alois Waldburg-Zeil as its new chief executive officer, effective April 1.

Waldburg-Zeil will succeed Peter Schildknecht, who will step down by the end of March, according to a Wednesday release.

data.title: Chinese Shares End Higher Amid Continued Positive Sentiment — Market Talk
data.description: Chinese shares closed higher, supported by semiconductor and technology stocks. Investor sentiment remains boosted after President Xi's meeting with business leaders, as markets await the Two Sessions meeting in early March for more policy signs. Among the top gainers, Will Semiconductor rose 10%, National Silicon Industry gained 15% and Shenzhen Inovance Technology was up 8.6%. Meanwhile, bank and pharmaceutical stocks led losses. Bank of Ningbo fell 2.0% and Sichuan Kelun Pharmaceutical closed 1.9% lower. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index closed 0.8% higher at 3351.76, the Shenzhen Composite Index gained 1.9% and the ChiNext Price Index rose 2.1%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Kitron Wins Order to Supply Electronics for Kongsberg Gruppen Unit's Joint Strike Missile
data.description:
Kitron secured an order worth 73 million Norwegian kroner to supply electronics for a joint strike missile to Kongsberg Gruppen's Defence & Aerospace unit.

The electronics manufacturer will begin deliveries in 2026, with the manufacturing to take place at its Arendal facility in Norway, according to a Tuesday release.

data.title: UK Producer Prices Rise More Than Expected
data.description:
Factory gate prices for UK-manufactured goods rose 0.3% year-on-year in January 2025, rebounding from a revised 0.1% decline in December and exceeding the expected 0.1% increase.

Out of 10 product groups, 9 contributed to the rise, with food products making the biggest impact (2.2% vs. 1.8% in December).

Prices also rose faster for chemicals and pharmaceuticals (1.5% vs. 0.6%) and textiles, apparel, and leather (4.5% vs. 4.2%).

Additionally, prices rebounded for alcoholic beverages and tobacco (0.3% vs. -1.5%), while declines in coke and refined petroleum products eased (-14.1% vs. -18.3%).

On a monthly basis, factory gate prices climbed 0.5%, the biggest increase since September 2023, following a 0.2% drop in December and surpassing forecasts of a 0.2% rise.

data.title: The Hour Glass Repurchases More Shares
data.description:
The Hour Glass bought back 2,700 shares in the open market on Tuesday for SG$4,258 or SG$1.56 apiece, according to a same-day filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The luxury watch retail group has been authorized to buy back 64.8 million shares under its current mandate. To date, it has bought back 521,000 shares.

data.title: Tokyo Most Actives
Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0715 GMT - German 10-year Bund yields look stable around 2.50%, while narrowing French OAT and Italian BTP spreads over Bunds signal hopes of joint European debt issuance, says Commerzbank Research's Michael Leister in a note. "The direction of travel remains clear as the ongoing bid in BTPs and OATs at the cyclical lows versus Bunds signals mutualization hopes," the head of interest rates strategy says. Meanwhile, the EU is considering to boost defense budgets in an effort to continue to support Ukraine. The 10-year OAT-Bund yield spread is 67 basis points, the lowest since late July, while the 10-year BTP-Bund yield spread is just below 106 basis points, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year Bund yield is up 1 basis point at 2.493%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0709 GMT - Europe's zippy stock market might point to hope that current travails will catalyse bolder action on the continent, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note. The Stoxx Europe 600 has hit new heights this year, driven by defense stocks amid plans for a step-up in military spending. That buoyancy might seem odd given Europe's fiscal, political and trade problems, Vistesen and Debono say. But those challenges might precisely be so great that investors see drastic action as the only option in Europe's capitals, they say. "[B]etting on Europe to get its act together means betting on stronger economic and political integration, and fiscal stimulus," the economists say. "Lots of fiscal stimulus." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0706 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower as concerns about borrowing costs and U.S. tariffs persisted. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 0.3% to 39164.61. Auto stocks and trading houses led declines. Honda Motor shed 2.3% and Toyota Motor fell 1.7% after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% or more on automobiles. Itochu dropped 2.6% and Mitsubishi Corp. declined 1.8%. The 10-year Japanese government bond yield rose 0.5 basis point to 1.435%, the highest level since November 2009. U.S. tariff and foreign policies are in focus. USD/JPY is at 151.63, compared with 152.06 as of Tuesday 5 p.m. Eastern time. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0659 GMT - The car industry is back in focus after President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% on autos, semiconductors and pharmaceuticals. Analysts at Nomura noted that the U.S. is the largest export market outside of the eurozone for German car manufacturers, and Trump has taken particular issue with the European automotive sector and Europeans not buying U.S. cars. "Some car manufacturers already forewarned factory closures and layoffs prior to Trump's election in November 2024, and the prospect of U.S. tariffs only worsens the situation," they write. Nomura also expects the U.S. administration to apply reciprocal tariffs sometime in April. Currently, the U.S. has a 2.5% tariff on European cars, while the EU charges U.S. imports 10%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - U.S. fiscal policy is core to Deutsche Bank Research's rates forecasts for this year, its strategists say in a note. They expect a modest net fiscal easing but with unusually elevated uncertainty related to both taxes and spending, they say. Currently, however, they don't yet see anything in the data that suggests federal spending has been put on a significantly different trajectory. Ten-year U.S. Treasury yields trade within their recent range, last little changed at 4.546%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi rose 1.7% to close at 2671.52, extending gains for a seventh consecutive session. Semiconductor and battery stocks led the gains. Foreign and institutional investors were net buyers. Chip stock gains on Wall Street overnight supported investor sentiment. Memory-chip makers Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix rose 3.2% and 4.0%, respectively. Samsung's planned share cancellation also kept the index-heavyweight stock higher for a second consecutive session. Electric-vehicle battery maker LG Energy Solution added 4.4%. USD/KRW settled 0.4% lower at 1,438.50 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was up 0.1 basis point at 2.879%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0649 GMT - The improved investor assessment seen in Germany's ZEW sentiment index released Tuesday in part "reflects increased expectations of ongoing European Central Bank rate cuts," say Daiwa Capital Markets Research analysts. Rate cuts are likely to go "perhaps into accommodative territory," they say in a note. Investors' expectations for the next six months rose 15.7 points on the month in February to 26.0, the indicator's biggest increase in two years. "And with luck, that improved investor mood will translate itself into a pickup in business survey indicators, such as the Ifo indices as well as firmer demand in the real economy," they added. Money markets price in three more ECB rate cuts of 25 bps each this year, bringing the deposit rate to 2.00% by September, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0635 GMT - BlackRock goes tactically--or short-term--overweight eurozone government bonds, reinforcing its preference for them due to tariff risks, it says in a note. Meanwhile, it stays underweight long-term U.S. Treasurys, it says. Persistent deficits and inflation in the U.S. make BlackRock more positive on fixed-income assets elsewhere, notably Europe, it says. U.S. President Trump has signaled potential tariffs on Europe. Europe's reliance on the U.S. as an export destination means tariffs would hurt the eurozone's growth more than it boosts inflation, BlackRock says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0631 GMT - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand's dovish guidance indicates potential for further, faster cuts through 2025, but there is a risk of a slowdown in the pace of easing too, Nomura analysts say. RBNZ guidance on the official cash rate signals scope for cuts through 2025 if conditions evolve as projected, Yusuke Miyairi and others say. They forecast 50bp and 25bp cuts in April and May, but see room for a more hawkish RBNZ. Asked about the pace of future cuts, Gov. Orr mentioned that the bank assumes 25bp cuts in April and May, they note, adding that the OCR is now 25bp above the upper bound of the estimated neutral range. U.S. tariff uncertainty may also lead to a more cautious approach if there is a larger-than-expected adverse growth impact, they add. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0617 GMT - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand's latest rate cut could well be the last 50-basis-point reduction it makes this cycle, with the size of cuts potentially decreasing from here, UOB economist Lee Sue Ann says. The RBNZ reiterated its expectation that the economy will recover modestly this year, with annual headline inflation forecast to remain on target, she says in a note. It suggested further scope to lower the official cash rate, with the latest forward guidance signaling the average OCR falling to 3.14% by December, Lee says. "With the OCR now much closer to neutral and the economy recovering slowly, we expect a more cautious RBNZ from here." For now, UOB looks for a further 75 bps of rate cuts at a 25bp clip for the rest of the year. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0514 GMT - The Reserve Bank of New Zealand slashed interest rates and lowered its projections for the path of the official cash rate for the year at its policy meeting. However, the central bank could end up cutting even more, says Jarrod Kerr, chief economist at Kiwibank. Kerr sees risks tilted to the downside for the OCR, as unemployment is still rising and inflation is stable. If anything, the RBNZ may need to stimulate more, by putting the economy in drive and tapping the accelerator, cutting the OCR below 3% from 3.75% now, Kerr adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.description: German 10-year Bund yields look stable around 2.50%, while narrowing French OAT and Italian BTP spreads over Bunds signal hopes of joint European debt issuance, says Commerzbank Research's Michael Leister in a note. "The direction of travel remains clear as the ongoing bid in BTPs and OATs at the cyclical lows versus Bunds signals mutualization hopes," the head of interest rates strategy says. Meanwhile, the EU is considering to boost defense budgets in an effort to continue to support Ukraine. The 10-year OAT-Bund yield spread is 67 basis points, the lowest since late July, while the 10-year BTP-Bund yield spread is just below 106 basis points, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year Bund yield is up 1 basis point at 2.493%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Pretax Pft GBP2.33B
BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Oper Pft GBP2.69B
BAE SYSTEMS PLC FY Net Pft GBP1.96B
